tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia based on classic clinical features and exclusion of red flags.

nl_assertions: Correctly identifies trigeminal neuralgia as the most likely diagnosis when pain is unilateral, brief, electric-shock/stabbing, in a trigeminal nerve distribution, triggered by light touch/chewing/talking, with pain-free intervals and normal neurologic exam.
Explains the diagnosis using the terms "trigeminal neuralgia" and "nerve pain" and frames it as paroxysmal facial pain from irritation/dysfunction of the trigeminal nerve.

Assesses for and documents key red flags/secondary causes that would change management (e.g., sensory loss or persistent numbness, bilateral symptoms, age <40, progressive course, hearing/vestibular symptoms, facial weakness, rash/vesicles, systemic symptoms, dental infection signs).

Includes an appropriate differential diagnosis (e.g., dental pathology, temporomandibular disorder, trigeminal neuropathy/postherpetic neuralgia, sinusitis, cluster headache, giant cell arteritis if age >50 with new headache/scalp tenderness/jaw claudication, intracranial mass/MS) and explains why trigeminal neuralgia is favored.
Avoids prescribing in an L1 task; if treatment is discussed, it remains educational and does not issue a prescription via tools.
If discussing treatment options, mentions carbamazepine as first-line for trigeminal neuralgia and uses the terms "trigeminal neuralgia" and "carbamazepine" in the treatment explanation milestone.

When discussing any potential medication, explicitly performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check, drug interaction check, and contraindication check (even if no prescription is ultimately made in this L1 task).

Provides safety-net advice: seek urgent care for new neurologic deficits, persistent facial numbness, severe continuous pain without breaks, fever/swelling suggesting infection, vesicular rash, vision symptoms, or inability to eat/drink.

Plans appropriate next steps for typical trigeminal neuralgia (e.g., recommend MRI brain with attention to posterior fossa/trigeminal nerve when indicated to exclude secondary causes; consider neurology referral if atypical features, poor control, or diagnostic uncertainty) without over-escalation for a straightforward case.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose classic trigeminal neuralgia based on hallmark paroxysmal, triggerable unilateral facial pain and no neurologic deficits.
must_collect: paroxysmal duration (seconds to minutes) with pain-free intervals
unilateral facial distribution consistent with V2/V3 (or V1) territory
trigger factors (light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind)
absence of persistent numbness or sensory loss
absence of dental infection symptoms (tooth pain with percussion, swelling)
basic neurologic red flags (weakness, diplopia, ataxia, vision loss)
age of onset and whether symptoms are bilateral
steps: Elicit pain features, distribution, triggers, and episodic pattern.
Screen for red flags/secondary causes and perform/describe focused cranial nerve and facial sensory exam as normal in typical cases.
State primary diagnosis: trigeminal neuralgia; briefly justify with classic features.
Provide patient-friendly explanation including "trigeminal neuralgia" and "nerve pain".
Discuss first-line treatment conceptually (include "carbamazepine" for "trigeminal neuralgia") while noting safety checks are required before any prescribing.
Give safety-net instructions and outline next steps (consider imaging/neurology if atypical or red flags).
Record diagnosis via tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia while explicitly documenting a brief differential and why alternatives are less likely (e.g., dental/TMJ/postherpetic neuralgia).
steps: Characterize pain (paroxysmal, shock-like), triggers, and trigeminal distribution.
Ask targeted questions to rule out dental pathology, TMJ dysfunction, sinusitis, herpes zoster, and continuous neuropathic pain.
Screen for red flags and secondary causes (bilateral symptoms, sensory deficits, age <40, progressive course).
Explain diagnosis using required terms; provide reassurance about benign nature but acknowledge severe pain.
Provide treatment education including carbamazepine for trigeminal neuralgia and note allergy/interaction/contraindication checks before prescribing.
Document trigeminal neuralgia diagnosis with supporting rationale.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia and recommend further evaluation for secondary causes when atypical features are present (e.g., sensory changes or age <40), without prescribing.
steps: Elicit pain pattern and triggers; identify atypical features (e.g., numbness, bilateral pain, prolonged attacks, continuous baseline pain).
Explain that symptoms may represent trigeminal neuralgia but could be secondary; recommend MRI and neurology evaluation.
Communicate diagnosis and treatment education milestones (mention carbamazepine as typical first-line) while deferring prescribing pending safety checks and evaluation.
Provide clear return precautions and document the working diagnosis.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia (>=3 nights/week for >=3 months) and the concept of impaired daytime functioning.

Screens for secondary causes and mimics before finalizing treatment: depression/anxiety/PTSD, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, pain, GERD, thyroid symptoms, restless legs/PLMD, and obstructive sleep apnea risk (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness).
Reviews current medications/supplements and substances that worsen sleep (stimulants, decongestants, corticosteroids, caffeine, nicotine, alcohol) and addresses sleep hygiene contributors.
Explains treatment including "CBT-I" as first-line and describes core components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, and sleep hygiene).

Discusses "sedatives" as second-line/adjunct, used only when indicated, at the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration, and includes risks (falls, cognitive impairment, dependence, next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors).

Performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: confirms allergies (allergy_check), checks for drug-drug interactions (drug_interaction_check), and evaluates contraindications (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, severe COPD/OSA risk, history of substance use disorder, older age/fall risk, hepatic impairment, and concurrent CNS depressants.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not recommend combining sedatives with alcohol/opioids/other CNS depressants; avoids long-term hypnotic therapy without reassessment; avoids benzodiazepines in high-risk patients (e.g., elderly, SUD history) unless strongly justified.

Provides clear patient-centered counseling: sets expectations on timeline for CBT-I benefit, provides written/structured plan, and advises when to seek urgent care (suicidal ideation, severe depression/mania, dangerous parasomnias, severe daytime somnolence affecting driving).

Prescribe baclofen with titration and safety counseling; include advice on stretching, heat, and trigger avoidance.
Plan follow-up for dose adjustment and monitoring of sedation/falls.

acceptable_variants.description: If patient declines baclofen after counseling, agent still meets L2 outcome by prescribing but documents shared decision-making and provides PT-focused plan (benchmark can accept if baclofen is offered and prescription is prepared/placed per task requirements).
steps: Explain HSP diagnosis and genetic testing.
Explain treatment including baclofen and physical therapy; explore preferences/concerns.
Perform allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks prior to placing prescription.
Place baclofen prescription with instructions but document patient preference to defer initiation; emphasize PT and reassess later.
Arrange follow-up to revisit medication initiation and response.
